summary: Dan Shapiro explains why investors refuse to meet with companies that compete with their existing portfolio investments. Drawing from his experience on both sides of the fundraising table, he dismantles three common entrepreneur objections: that the companies aren't really competing, that an initial conversation would clarify things, and that the entrepreneur doesn't mind shared investors. He then reveals the deeper reason: even without any wrongdoing, an investor who meets with competitors risks reputation damage and legal exposure when portfolio companies inevitably converge on similar ideas.
key_points: Entrepreneurs systematically overestimate how different they are from competitors because they only know as much as the last press release
Investors are looking for reasons to say no, not reasons to say yes — a potential conflict is efficient triage
The real risk is reputational and legal: when two companies independently converge on the same idea, the investor gets blamed for leaking information
Fiduciary obligations create impossible conflicts when an investor holds confidential information from competing companies
Conflict is subjective and expands over time as companies pivot, making conservative avoidance the rational default
money_quotes: I didn't actually grok what was going on until I sat on the other side, looking at investments.
Telling an investor that there's no conflict is like telling them what they should eat for dinner – no matter how much you know about the situation, they know more.
Most investors are looking for reasons to say no.
A typical VC is going to say 'yes' to between 1% and 0.1% of pitches; that means they need to triage opportunities aggressively, and guess what? You Just Got Triaged.
Good investors feel a strong obligation to their existing portfolio, and they don't owe you squat.
Your investor chose their horse because they thought it could beat all comers, including your horse. Placing a second bet jeopardizes them both legally and ethically.
key_insight: Investors avoid competitors not out of closed-mindedness but because even innocent exposure to competing pitches creates unavoidable reputational and legal risk when companies inevitably converge on similar ideas.
